title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a sound designer called Noah Fletcher talking about his work creating sound effects for major films. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a sound designer called Noah Fletcher talking about his work creating sound effects for major films. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Noah Fletcher explains the techniques behind 'Foley' and how everyday objects create cinematic sounds.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Noah. My job is to make you believe that what you’re hearing on screen is real, even when it’s completely fake. I’m a Foley artist—the person who recreates everyday sounds for movies. Many people assume we have a massive digital library of sounds, and while we do use those, the most convincing effects are still made by hand in a studio. I first became interested in this field while I was doing a [7]summer internship[/7] at a local radio station. I watched an old-timer use a pair of shoes on a piece of gravel to simulate a character walking down a driveway, and I was mesmerized. 

The philosophy of Foley is simple: don't record what you see, record what it sounds like. For example, if you see a character walking through deep snow, we don't use real snow—it would melt and sound far too quiet. Instead, we use a [8]leather bag[/8] filled with cornstarch. When you squeeze it in a specific way, it produces that perfect 'crunch' that the audience expects. It’s all about finding the right texture, not the actual object shown on the screen.

One of the most challenging projects I ever worked on was a sci-fi epic where I had to create the sound of a giant alien creature breathing. I tried dozens of animal recordings, but they all sounded too familiar. Eventually, I discovered that a [9]heavy raincoat[/9] being dragged slowly across a marble floor created a rhythmic, wet sound that was incredibly unsettling. It’s often the most mundane items in your house that provide the most terrifying cinematic experiences.

In the studio, we have what we call 'pits'—large rectangular boxes filled with different materials like sand, gravel, wood, or even water. Keeping these pits in good condition is vital. I found that the [10]humidity level[/10] in the room is the biggest factor in how these materials sound. If the sand gets too dry, it loses its 'weight'; if the wood gets too damp, it creaks in the wrong pitch. I spend the first hour of every day just calibrating the environment before a single microphone is turned on.

Precision is key in our industry. We have to perform our sounds in perfect sync with the actors on the screen. I think the most essential quality for a successful Foley artist is actually [11]physical coordination[/11]. You are essentially a dancer; you have to move your body and your props in time with the film's rhythm. If your footsteps are half a second off, the whole illusion is ruined. It takes years of practice to get that timing right.

People are often surprised by the equipment we use. While we have world-class microphones, our most valuable tool is often something as simple as a [12]broken umbrella[/12]. It can sound like a bird’s wings flapping or a creaking door, depending on how you manipulate it. I have a whole shelf in my garage dedicated to 'junk' that I’ve picked up from various flea markets over the years. My wife calls it a mess; I call it my orchestra.

As technology advances, we are seeing a shift in how sound is processed. However, I’ve noticed a recent [13]industry trend[/13] where directors are moving back towards organic, handmade sounds. They feel that computer-generated effects can often sound 'sterile' or 'flat'. There’s an emotional depth to a real sound that a synthesizer just can’t replicate. It’s a great time to be a craftsman in this digital world.

Finally, for those looking to enter this profession, my advice is to start listening to the world around you. I’m currently writing a [14]practical guide[/14] for students, which I hope to publish next year. It focuses on how to build a home Foley studio on a budget. The most important thing is to never stop experimenting. You never know when a bag of frozen peas might become the sound of a rainstorm. Thanks for your attention.
